notes: Defender pass, 2 scoped claims. searches: ~4 (3 WebSearch + 1 successful WebFetch of D'Aubigne b.14; one CCEL fetch 404'd), all spent on GC-11-095 to settle the contradicted-vs-anachronistic question by checking White's own period source; GC-11-076 audited Read-only with 0 searches. full defenses: 1, cheap audits: 1. NOTE: chapter has 108 claims (<=120) but the verified.json is ~96k tokens, exceeding a single Write budget as with 176-claim ch06; delta format used so merge_stage.py injects these two defense objects into the validated full array without touching the 106 frozen claims.

GC-11-095.argument: Strongest steelman, three prongs. (1) Rhetorical/synecdochic reading. Para 35 opens a short ascending list of Catholic-side concessions that White has just built in para 34: an unnamed papist bishop ('All that the Lutherans have said is true; we cannot deny it', GC-11-091) and Doctor Eck's admission that he could not refute the Confession from apostles and prophets (GC-11-092). Read in that flow, 'The emperor himself declared that the Protestant articles were but the truth' functions as the climax ('even the emperor') of a genre of private-concession anecdotes, and could be defended as a compressed restatement that the imperial party itself conceded the Confession's biblical grounding rather than a literal minute of a public imperial pronouncement. (2) Documented kernel. Charles V did NOT dismiss the Confession out of hand: contemporaries report he listened attentively, he kept the Latin copy, he found the first draft of the Confutatio too intemperate and sent it back repeatedly for revision, and he pursued reconciliation negotiations for months. So there is a real historical substratum of the emperor treating the articles with unusual seriousness that White's sentence exaggerates. (3) Anachronism-tracing attempt. Because the whole Augsburg narrative in this chapter faithfully follows D'Aubigne b.14 (fn 300-306), if D'Aubigne attributed such a concession to the emperor the correct verdict would be anachronistic, not contradicted. WHY THE DEFENSE FAILS: The sentence carries no footnote (para 35 is White's own uncited summary; fn 306 = D'Aubigne b.14 ch.8 closes at para 34), and my check of D'Aubigne's Book 14 turned up no statement attributing to Charles V any declaration that the articles were true - the favorable-reaction anecdotes there attach to Duke William of Bavaria and to Eck, not to the emperor, and D'Aubigne shows Charles 'astounded at their firmness', keeping the copy, begging them not to publish, and finally giving up hope of reconciliation. So the anachronism downgrade cannot be grounded: this is White's own over-generalization, not a faithful rendering of a named period source's claim about the emperor. Meanwhile the plain, personal reading is positively contradicted by the record: Charles officially adopted the Catholic Confutatio (3 Aug 1530) condemning 13 articles, and the diet's verdict of 19 Nov 1530 held the Lutheran doctrine disproved and demanded recantation within six months. The synecdoche reading is too strained to carry 'the emperor himself declared', and the documented kernel (serious, non-dismissive treatment) is not the same as declaring the articles 'but the truth'. Contradicted stands.

new_sources.title: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation in the 16th Century, Book 14 (The Augsburg Confession, 1530) - full text reproduction
new_sources.note: White's own period source: no declaration by Charles V that the articles were true; emperor shown astounded at Protestant firmness, keeping the Latin copy, begging them not to publish, and abandoning hope of reconciliation. Concession anecdotes attach to Duke William of Bavaria and Eck, not the emperor - so the claim cannot be reclassified as anachronistic.

new_sources.title: The Augsburg Confession - Book of Concord (historical introduction)
new_sources.note: Documents Charles V's rejection via the Confutatio and the recantation demand; the favorable remark of Duke William of Bavaria ('Never before has this doctrine been presented to me in this manner') is the concession tradition, and it belongs to the duke, not the emperor.

GC-11-076.argument: Cheap Read-only audit (0 searches). The anachronistic call is sound and internally consistent, and the strongest available steelman does not overturn it. Steelman: the claim is compound and its core is accurate - Luther genuinely accompanied the elector's party as far as Coburg on the 1530 journey to Augsburg and remained there during the Diet, and reviving the party's spirits by singing an existing hymn is entirely possible since 'Ein feste Burg' was already in circulation by 1529; the whole scene faithfully renders White's cited source (para 29 draws on D'Aubigne b.14 ch.2, fn 300). Only the parenthetical 'written on that journey' is false: the hymn appears in the Wittenberg hymnals of 1528/1529 and is dated by modern scholarship to c.1527-1529, so it demonstrably predates the 1530 Coburg journey and cannot have been composed on it. An over-literalization defense (reading 'that journey' loosely) does not work, because the sentence unambiguously ties composition to the 1530 trip. Nor does a 'disputed' downgrade fit: although the hymn's exact composition date is itself debated (estimates 1521-1529), 1530 lies OUTSIDE that scholarly range - it is positively excluded by the 1528/1529 printing - so this is not genuine ongoing disagreement but a datable error. And the error traces precisely to D'Aubigne's now-rejected tradition tying the hymn to the 1530 Augsburg journey, which White reproduced. That is the textbook profile of 'anachronistic': wrong by modern standards, faithful to the 19th-century source White used. The verifier's sources (A Mighty Fortress - Wikipedia; Hymnology Archive) support the earlier dating and the D'Aubigne attribution; nothing in the audit undermines them, so no search lead was triggered. Verdict confirmed as anachronistic; the discrepancy (the composition-date component only) stands.
